context:
Page 31, Section 7 (Future Research Directions), first bullet.

We showed that every symmetric convex body can be approximated arbitrarily well by a GE. Our approximation factor relies on results on polytopic approximation of convex bodies. A related question is: how well can one approximate an n-dimensional symmetric convex body with a finite intersection of m (co-centered) ellipsoids, with m growing potentially with n?
title: Approximation of symmetric convex bodies by intersections of co-centered ellipsoids
background_latex: Let \(n\ge 1\). A set \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) is a symmetric convex body if it is compact, convex, has nonempty interior, and satisfies \(x\in C\Rightarrow -x\in C\); assume it is centered at the origin. An ellipsoid centered at the origin is a set \(E(Q):=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n\mid x^TQx\le 1\}\) for some matrix \(Q\in\mathbb{S}^n_{++}\). For an integer \(m\ge 1\), an intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids is a set of the form \(E:=\bigcap_{j=1}^m E(Q_j)\) with \(Q_j\in\mathbb{S}^n_{++}\). For \(\alpha\ge 1\), write \(\alpha E:=\{\alpha x\mid x\in E\}\).
problem_definition_latex:
Problem: Determine, as a function of \(n\) and \(m\), the smallest approximation factor \(\alpha(n,m)\ge 1\) such that for every symmetric convex body \(C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) there exist \(Q_1,\dots,Q_m\in\mathbb{S}^n_{++}\) with \(E:=\bigcap_{j=1}^m\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n\mid x^TQ_jx\le 1\}\) satisfying \[E\subseteq C\subseteq \alpha(n,m)\,E.\]
Equivalently, give asymptotically tight upper and lower bounds on \(\alpha(n,m)\) (or on the required \(m\) to achieve a target factor \(1+\varepsilon\)).
